category.label: Linear Equations and Inequalities
category.description: Solving, rearranging, and interpreting one-variable linear equations and inequalities, including multi-step forms, literal equations, compound inequalities, and solution-set notation. Literal-equation fluency is prerequisite for parameter extraction in all downstream function-modeling topics.
category.yield_rationale: Medium yield: solving linear equations feeds exponential/log/trig equation solving (2.13, 3.10), and literal-equation fluency is critical for extracting sinusoidal and exponential model parameters in all three AP Precalc units; interval notation is the language of domain/range throughout.

umbrellas.label: Linear Equation Foundations: Core Concepts
umbrellas.description: Fundamental structural concepts behind one-variable linear equations: equality meaning, solution definition and verification, equivalent equations, balance principle, and inverse operations.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Foundational literacy; equality properties and isolation concept are background assumed by all downstream algebra but rarely the direct test object once functions are introduced.

in_depth.label: Equation statement structure
in_depth.description: Recognizes an equation as a statement that two expressions have equal value; identifies the left side, right side, and equals sign before any solving begins.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Pure structural literacy; prerequisite knowledge assumed in all later solving but not directly tested.

in_depth.label: Meaning of a linear equation solution
in_depth.description: Distinguishes solving from rewriting; identifies that a solution is a value making the equation true and that most one-variable linear equations have exactly one solution.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Conceptual prerequisite; tested early but not revisited as a direct question type once solving is fluent.

in_depth.label: Solution verification for linear equations
in_depth.description: Checks a proposed solution by substituting it into both sides and comparing; targets errors where a value is produced but not validated against the original equation.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Verification strategy is exercised inside exponential and trig equation solving downstream but as a sub-step, not the focal skill.

in_depth.label: Equivalent linear equations
in_depth.description: Identifies whether two equations have the same solution set; recognizes that valid transformations preserve solutions while changing the equation's form.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Equivalence reasoning is implicit in all solving but rarely the isolated question target.

in_depth.label: Balance principle for equations
in_depth.description: States that applying the same valid operation to both sides of an equation preserves equality; this is the structural rationale behind every algebraic solving step.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Conceptual anchor for all solving; not independently tested at AP level but is the diagnostic skill that catches one-sided operation errors.

in_depth.label: Inverse operations in linear equations
in_depth.description: Identifies and applies inverse operations (addition-subtraction pair, multiplication-division pair) to undo operations on the variable and achieve isolation.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Inverse-operation reasoning generalizes directly to undoing exponential, logarithmic, and trigonometric operations in downstream units.

umbrellas.label: Linear Equation Foundations: Properties and Solution Sets
umbrellas.description: Properties of equality (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division), variable isolation as a goal, and solution-set representation for one-variable linear equations.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Properties of equality are the step-by-step justification behind solving; solution-set representation feeds downstream notation work.

in_depth.label: Addition property of equality
in_depth.description: Adding the same quantity to both sides of an equation creates an equivalent equation; used to undo subtraction when isolating the variable.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Routine step in all multi-step solving; not independently high-yield but failures here propagate to every solving task.

in_depth.label: Subtraction property of equality
in_depth.description: Subtracting the same quantity from both sides of an equation creates an equivalent equation; used to undo addition when isolating the variable.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Same load-bearing role as addition property; an arithmetic sub-step rather than an independent exam target.

in_depth.label: Multiplication property of equality
in_depth.description: Multiplying both sides by the same nonzero quantity creates an equivalent equation; used to clear fractional coefficients or undo division.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Slightly higher than addition/subtraction because it involves the nonzero restriction and fractional-coefficient handling that reappear in literal equations.

in_depth.label: Division property of equality
in_depth.description: Dividing both sides by the same nonzero quantity creates an equivalent equation; used to undo multiplication by a coefficient in the final isolation step.
in_depth.yield_rationale: The most-used final step in linear equation solving; appears ubiquitously in literal equations and formula rearrangement.

in_depth.label: Variable isolation
in_depth.description: Identifies isolation as the goal of solving a one-variable linear equation; recognizes when a variable is or is not alone on one side of the equals sign.
in_depth.yield_rationale: The goal-state concept behind all solving; prerequisite for literal-equation rearrangement which is highly load-bearing.

in_depth.label: Single-value solution sets
in_depth.description: Represents the solution of a typical one-variable linear equation as a singleton set or a single statement; distinguishes one solution from no solution or all real numbers.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Notation literacy; not a frequently isolated exam question but necessary for solution-set communication.

in_depth.label: Variable terms on equation sides
in_depth.description: Recognizes whether variable terms appear on the left side only, the right side only, or both sides of an equation, guiding which moves are needed before isolating.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Structural scanning skill that precedes variable-collection steps; moderate diagnostic value.

in_depth.label: Zero variable coefficient after simplification
in_depth.description: Recognizes that when variable terms cancel during simplification to produce 0·x, the equation has no variable and is either always true (identity) or always false (contradiction); identifies this structural fork before any isolation step is attempted.
in_depth.yield_rationale: The structural fork that explains both special cases; diagnostic value for recognizing when the variable disappears.

umbrellas.label: One-Step Linear Equations: Basic Operations
umbrellas.description: One-variable linear equations solvable with a single inverse operation using integer, negative, and zero-solution structures; covers addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, and negative coefficients.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: The one-step case is the terminal step in every more complex solving chain; basic operation fluency rarely the stand-alone AP target but errors propagate widely.

in_depth.label: One-step equations with addition
in_depth.description: Solves x + a = b by subtracting the same constant from both sides; targets the form where addition is attached directly to the variable.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Elementary case; mastered early and rarely the sole question target in AP-level work.

in_depth.label: One-step equations with subtraction
in_depth.description: Solves x − a = b by adding the same constant to both sides; targets the form where subtraction follows the variable.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Parallel to addition case; elementary and foundational.

in_depth.label: One-step equations with variable subtracted from a constant
in_depth.description: Solves a − x = b, requiring recognition that the variable carries an implicit coefficient of −1 and handling distinct from x − a = b.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Slightly higher than basic addition/subtraction because the implied negative coefficient is a persistent error source across many equation types.

in_depth.label: One-step equations with multiplication
in_depth.description: Solves ax = b by dividing both sides by the nonzero coefficient; targets integer coefficient structures.
in_depth.yield_rationale: The division-to-isolate step appears in virtually every solving chain; foundational.

in_depth.label: One-step equations with division
in_depth.description: Solves x/a = b by multiplying both sides by the nonzero divisor; targets variables in numerator over a constant.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Reciprocal-multiplication step is the foundation for clearing fractional coefficients in literal equations.

in_depth.label: One-step equations with negative coefficients
in_depth.description: Solves −ax = b by dividing by a negative number; isolates sign errors that arise when the coefficient is negative.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Sign errors in negative-coefficient equations are among the most common arithmetic mistakes; reappear throughout all subsequent solving contexts.

umbrellas.label: One-Step Linear Equations: Fractions, Decimals, and Special Cases
umbrellas.description: One-step equations with fractional or decimal coefficients, non-integer solutions, zero solutions, and non-standard variable positions.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Fraction and decimal coefficient cases have higher downstream relevance in literal equations and applied modeling; special-case recognition prevents specific persistent misconceptions.

in_depth.label: One-step equations with fraction coefficients
in_depth.description: Solves (a/b)x = c by multiplying by the reciprocal b/a; focuses on fractional coefficient structure as distinct from variable-in-denominator rational equations.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Reciprocal-multiplication fluency directly enables literal-equation rearrangement for function parameters.

in_depth.label: One-step equations with decimal coefficients
in_depth.description: Solves 0.px = q by dividing by the decimal coefficient or converting to a fraction; targets coefficient interpretation in decimal form.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Decimal arithmetic sub-skill; moderate frequency in applied function contexts.

in_depth.label: One-step equations with fraction solutions
in_depth.description: Solves integer-coefficient equations like 4x = 7 whose solutions are non-integer fractions; targets the misconception that simple equations always produce integer answers.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Fraction solutions appear routinely when solving for exponential or trigonometric model parameters.

in_depth.label: One-step equations with zero solution
in_depth.description: Recognizes and accepts x = 0 as a valid unique solution; distinguishes zero as a solution from a no-solution case.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Conceptual clarification; low independent frequency but eliminates a specific misconception.

in_depth.label: One-step equations with variable on the right
in_depth.description: Solves equations such as 15 = x + 6 where the variable is on the right side; tests that isolation logic is not position-dependent.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Structural flexibility; reappears in formula rearrangement when the target variable is on the non-standard side.

in_depth.label: One-step equations with signed constants
in_depth.description: Solves one-step equations containing negative constants or subtracting a negative, such as x + (−6) = 11 or a − (−4) = 9; targets signed-number errors in constant handling.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Signed-constant errors propagate into all higher-difficulty equation solving; persistent error source.

umbrellas.label: Two-Step Linear Equations: Core Forms
umbrellas.description: One-variable linear equations requiring exactly two inverse operations; covers standard forms, non-standard term order, negative variable terms, and variable-divided-by-constant structures.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: The two-step structure is the most frequently encountered equation form in applied algebra; mastery directly reduces error rates in multi-step and literal equation solving.

in_depth.label: Two-step equations: ax + b = c
in_depth.description: Solves the standard two-step form ax + b = c by first subtracting b from both sides and then dividing by a; requires correct reversal of operation order.
in_depth.yield_rationale: The canonical two-step equation form; appears as a sub-task in virtually every linear formula rearrangement.

in_depth.label: Two-step equations: ax − b = c
in_depth.description: Solves ax − b = c by adding b to both sides before dividing by a; distinguishes the sign of the constant addend.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Subtracted-constant form is slightly more error-prone than added-constant; equal frequency in practice.

in_depth.label: Two-step equations with constant before variable term
in_depth.description: Solves equations such as 4 + 2x = 18 where the constant is written before the variable term; tests order-invariant application of inverse operations.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Parsing equations with non-standard term order is a recurring structural skill.

in_depth.label: Two-step equations with negative variable term
in_depth.description: Solves equations such as −3x + 8 = 20 where the variable coefficient is negative; requires sign tracking after the constant term is moved.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Negative variable terms are a top error source; this structure appears in sinusoidal and linear model inversion tasks.

in_depth.label: Two-step equations: x/a + b = c
in_depth.description: Solves x/a + b = c by first removing the constant and then multiplying by the divisor; the undoing of division requires multiplication as the inverse.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Quotient-variable structure appears in rate and proportion applications and in literal equations.

in_depth.label: Reverse order of operations in two-step equations
in_depth.description: Applies inverse operations in the correct reverse order: undo the constant first (addition/subtraction), then undo the coefficient (multiplication/division).
in_depth.yield_rationale: Correct reversal of operation order generalizes to exponential-to-log and log-to-exponential equation solving sequences in Units 2 and 3.

umbrellas.label: Two-Step Linear Equations: Fractions, Decimals, and Special Cases
umbrellas.description: Two-step equations with fractional or decimal coefficients, negative and zero solutions, right-side variable expressions, and parenthesized constants.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Fractional and decimal coefficient handling extends directly to literal-equation rearrangement for function formulas; special-case solutions build solution-set literacy.

in_depth.label: Two-step equations with fraction coefficients
in_depth.description: Solves (a/b)x + c = d by isolating the variable term and then multiplying by the reciprocal; tests reciprocal use within a two-step structure.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Fractional coefficient handling is directly required in literal equation rearrangement for function formulas (yield 0.80 downstream).

in_depth.label: Two-step equations with decimal coefficients
in_depth.description: Solves two-step equations with decimal variable coefficients such as 0.5x + 4 = 10; may solve directly with decimal arithmetic or convert the coefficient first.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Decimal coefficients appear in applied exponential modeling contexts; moderate direct frequency.

in_depth.label: Two-step equations with negative solutions
in_depth.description: Recognizes and correctly interprets negative solutions in two-step equations; targets sign errors in final answers from signed constants or negative coefficients.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Negative solutions are common in function-model parameter extraction; errors here directly cause parameter sign mistakes.

in_depth.label: Two-step equations with zero solution
in_depth.description: Identifies that x = 0 is a valid unique solution when a two-step equation is solved; avoids confusing zero-as-answer with no-solution.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Conceptual clarification; occurs in specific equation structures but low overall frequency.

in_depth.label: Two-step equations with variable expression on the right
in_depth.description: Solves equations such as 19 = 4x + 3 where the two-step variable expression is on the right side; tests position-independent isolation logic.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Right-side variable form tests structural flexibility; moderate frequency in formula rearrangement.

in_depth.label: Two-step equations with signed constant grouping
in_depth.description: Interprets signed constants in parentheses such as x + (−5) or a − (−4) before applying two-step solving; targets grouping-notation errors.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Parenthesized sign notation appears in function expressions and transformation descriptions throughout the course.

umbrellas.label: Multi-Step Linear Equations: Distribution and Collection
umbrellas.description: Linear equations requiring distribution, like-term collection, and variables on both sides before isolation. Covers one-side and both-sides distribution, negative and subtracted groups, and variable collection.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Multi-step equation fluency is the core downstream skill referenced in the precalc-outline: it directly mirrors exponential and logarithmic equation solving and literal-equation rearrangement for model parameters.

in_depth.label: Linear equations requiring like-term collection on one side
in_depth.description: Collects like terms on one side before applying inverse operations; targets equations such as 3x + 4x − 5 = 16 where simplification precedes isolation.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Term collection is an obligatory sub-step in simplifying exponential and logarithmic equations after log rules are applied.

in_depth.label: Linear equations requiring distribution on one side
in_depth.description: Expands a grouped expression on one side using the distributive property before solving; targets structures such as 3(x + 4) = 21.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Distribution before solving mirrors the expansion required when applying logarithm or exponent rules in function equations.

in_depth.label: Linear equations requiring negative distribution
in_depth.description: Distributes a negative factor outside parentheses correctly before solving; targets sign errors in structures such as −2(x + 5) = 14.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Negative distribution errors are among the most persistent algebra mistakes; relevant to trig identity manipulation and log property application.

in_depth.label: Linear equations with subtracted parentheses
in_depth.description: Changes the sign of every term inside a subtracted group before collecting terms; targets equations such as x − (2x + 5) = 11.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Subtracted-group sign distribution is a precursor to the same skill needed when rewriting trig or log expressions.

in_depth.label: Linear equations with multiple grouped expressions
in_depth.description: Distributes across two or more grouped expressions on one or both sides before combining like terms; each group must be expanded before term collection.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Multiple-group expansion is the algebraic engine behind simplifying product-form trig and exponential equations.

in_depth.label: Linear equations with variables on both sides
in_depth.description: Moves variable terms to one side and constants to the other before isolating; targets equations such as 5x + 2 = 2x + 14.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Variables-on-both-sides structure appears directly in exponential/log equations after applying inverse operations and in sinusoidal parameter equations.

in_depth.label: Linear equations with constants on both sides
in_depth.description: Gathers constants on the side opposite the variable terms using addition or subtraction; targets equations such as 4x + 9 = 2x − 5.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Constant-gathering step is routinely required in function model construction involving two-point parameter extraction.

in_depth.label: Linear equations requiring distribution on both sides
in_depth.description: Expands grouped expressions on both sides before collecting terms; targets equations such as 3(x + 2) = 2(x + 9).
in_depth.yield_rationale: Full distribution on both sides is the most complex single-equation algebraic manipulation; prerequisite for symbolic FRQ manipulation tasks.

umbrellas.label: Multi-Step Linear Equations: Clearing and Special Cases
umbrellas.description: Linear equations requiring both-sides like-term collection, clearing of fraction or decimal coefficients using LCD or powers of 10, proportion and cross-product equations, and identification of no-solution and identity special cases.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Fraction-clearing and special-case recognition are high-load sub-skills in literal equations and model-parameter extraction; proportion equations appear in applied function problems.

in_depth.label: Linear equations requiring like-term collection on both sides
in_depth.description: Simplifies each side independently before moving terms across the equation; targets equations with multiple like terms on both sides before clearing or isolating.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Both-sides simplification is required in solving after applying log or exponent rules that produce multiple like terms.

in_depth.label: Multi-step linear equations with fraction coefficients
in_depth.description: Solves equations with fractional coefficients on both sides using fraction arithmetic or algebraic rearrangement; limited to constant denominators only.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Fraction-coefficient equations model the algebraic structure of rearranging formulas involving rational parameters in function modeling.

in_depth.label: Clearing fraction coefficients in linear equations
in_depth.description: Multiplies every term by the least common denominator to remove constant fraction denominators, then solves the resulting integer-coefficient equation.
in_depth.yield_rationale: LCD-clearing strategy mirrors the algebraic manipulation used to isolate a variable in formulas with constant denominator parameters.

in_depth.label: Multi-step linear equations with decimal coefficients
in_depth.description: Solves equations with decimal variable and constant terms directly or by clearing decimals with a power of 10; targets equations such as 0.4x + 1.2 = 3.6.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Decimal-coefficient equations appear in applied exponential model fitting where calculated parameters are decimal values.

in_depth.label: Clearing decimal coefficients in linear equations
in_depth.description: Multiplies every term by a power of 10 to convert decimal coefficients to integers before solving; identifies the correct power of 10 to use.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Useful technique but less frequently required than fraction-clearing at AP level.

in_depth.label: Cross-product linear equations with constant denominators
in_depth.description: Applies cross multiplication when a proportion has constant denominators and a linear numerator to produce a solvable linear equation; excludes rational equations with variable denominators.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Cross-product setting up a linear equation reappears in rate-of-change and similar-figure proportion applications.

in_depth.label: Linear equations with no solution
in_depth.description: Identifies that an equation has no solution when variable terms cancel and a false constant statement results, such as 0 = 5.
in_depth.yield_rationale: No-solution recognition is directly applied when solving exponential equations or checking whether a model can be parameterized for given data.

in_depth.label: Linear equations with infinitely many solutions
in_depth.description: Identifies that an equation has infinitely many solutions when it reduces to a true identity such as 0 = 0; every real number satisfies the equation.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Identity recognition distinguishes dependent from consistent equations; relevant to model-parameter uniqueness analysis.

umbrellas.label: Literal Equations and Formula Rearrangement: Core Isolation
umbrellas.description: Isolating a specified variable in multi-variable equations using linear equation principles; covers single-operation isolation, two-step forms, and variable-on-both-sides collection.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Highest-yield umbrella in this category: extracting parameters A, B, C, D from sinusoidal models and a, b from exponential models is exactly literal-equation work; referenced explicitly in the precalc-outline as a 0.80 foundation yield.

in_depth.label: Literal equations isolating a variable with addition
in_depth.description: Isolates a specified variable by undoing addition or subtraction involving other variables or constants; targets symbolic isolation without numeric values.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Addition-based rearrangement is the basic step in isolating midline D from sinusoidal model form or base b from exponential form.

in_depth.label: Literal equations isolating a multiplied variable
in_depth.description: Isolates a variable multiplied by a nonzero quantity by dividing both sides; targets forms ax = b and ab = c solved symbolically for one factor.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Division to isolate a multiplied parameter is the core of extracting amplitude A or growth rate b from function formulas.

in_depth.label: Literal equations isolating a divided variable
in_depth.description: Isolates a variable in a numerator over a constant or parameter by multiplying both sides by the denominator; excludes solving for variables in denominators.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Clearing a constant denominator to isolate a numerator variable is directly required in rearranging period formulas B = 2π/P.

in_depth.label: Literal equations with two-step linear forms
in_depth.description: Isolates a specified variable in ax + b = c form with parameters; undoes addition/subtraction before undoing multiplication, mirroring two-step numeric solving.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Two-step literal form is the most common structure for extracting function parameters from a model equation such as y = A·b^x + c solved for x.

in_depth.label: Literal equations with the target variable on both sides
in_depth.description: Gathers all terms containing the target variable to one side before isolating; the equation is linear in the target variable even when other variables appear.
in_depth.yield_rationale: This structure appears when the same parameter appears on both sides of a model equation, requiring collection before isolation.

in_depth.label: Factoring out the target variable in literal equations
in_depth.description: After collecting terms with the target variable, factors the variable out of a sum before dividing; targets forms ax + bx = c solved for x via x(a + b) = c.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Factoring the target variable is the key step in solving systems of function equations and is flagged explicitly in the precalc-outline as a high-load literal-equation skill.

umbrellas.label: Literal Equations and Formula Rearrangement: Standard Formula Forms
umbrellas.description: Rearranging standard formulas and relations for a specified variable, including slope-intercept forms, fraction coefficient formulas, parenthesized formulas, subtracted-variable forms, and restriction identification.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Standard formula rearrangement is directly applied to sinusoidal, exponential, and linear function formulas throughout AP Precalc; restriction identification is required for domain analysis.

in_depth.label: Linear formula rearrangement for y
in_depth.description: Isolates y in a linear relation such as Ax + By = C; purely algebraic rearrangement without graphing or function interpretation.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Slope-intercept conversion is prerequisite for reading domain/range and interpreting graph behavior; medium-high direct use.

in_depth.label: Linear formula rearrangement for x
in_depth.description: Isolates x in a linear relation such as y = 2x + 7 or Ax + By = C; moves terms and divides by the coefficient symbolically.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Solving for x in a linear relation mirrors inverting a linear function; direct prerequisite for inverse-function reasoning in AP Precalc.

in_depth.label: Formula rearrangement with fraction coefficients
in_depth.description: Rearranges formulas whose target variable has a fractional coefficient by clearing denominators or multiplying by the reciprocal; targets slope-intercept and area formulas.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Fractional parameter rearrangement appears in A = (1/2)bh and similar geometry/physics formulas that arise in applied modeling.

in_depth.label: Formula rearrangement with parentheses
in_depth.description: Distributes or divides by a common factor in a formula containing grouped expressions to isolate the requested variable; targets forms such as P = 2(l + w).
in_depth.yield_rationale: Parenthesized formulas appear in standard geometric and sinusoidal model forms that students must rearrange during FRQ construction tasks.

in_depth.label: Formula rearrangement with subtracted target variable
in_depth.description: Handles the implied negative coefficient correctly when the target variable is subtracted, solving forms such as a − x = b for x.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Subtracted-variable forms arise in difference-based sinusoidal model rearrangements and are a reliable error source.

in_depth.label: Restrictions in formula rearrangement
in_depth.description: States the nonzero restriction on any parameter that becomes a divisor during isolation; identifies when a rearrangement is undefined.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Noting divisor restrictions is explicitly required in rational-function and domain analysis; moderate direct yield.

in_depth.label: Linear proportion equations with variable numerator
in_depth.description: Solves a proportion x/a = b/c where the variable is in the numerator using equivalent ratios or cross products; excludes variable-in-denominator rational equations.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Proportion solving with variable numerator is a common model-construction sub-task in applied function problems; placed here because proportions are a form of formula rearrangement.

umbrellas.label: Linear Inequality Foundations: Symbols, Properties, and Solution Meaning
umbrellas.description: Core structural concepts for one-variable linear inequalities: symbol meanings (strict and inclusive), solution set meaning and verification, equivalent inequalities, and all six properties of inequality.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Interval notation is the language of domain and range throughout AP Precalc Units 2 and 3; sign-reversal rules reappear implicitly whenever a negative coefficient is encountered in any solving context.

in_depth.label: Inequality statement structure
in_depth.description: Reads an inequality as a comparison of two expressions; identifies the inequality symbol, left side, and right side before any solving.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Structural literacy; prerequisite background not independently tested at AP level.

in_depth.label: Strict inequality symbols
in_depth.description: Recognizes that < and > exclude the boundary value, producing open endpoints and solution sets that do not include the boundary.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Strict vs. inclusive distinction drives domain boundary notation in rational and radical functions throughout AP Precalc.

in_depth.label: Inclusive inequality symbols
in_depth.description: Recognizes that ≤ and ≥ include the boundary value, producing closed endpoints and solution sets that include the boundary.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Same domain-notation importance as strict symbols; paired concept.

in_depth.label: Meaning of a linear inequality solution
in_depth.description: Identifies that a linear inequality solution is any value satisfying the comparison and that infinitely many values typically satisfy a one-variable linear inequality.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Conceptual pivot from equation (one solution) to inequality (ray of solutions); directly relevant to domain/range interpretation.

in_depth.label: Solution verification for linear inequalities
in_depth.description: Substitutes a candidate value into an inequality to check whether the comparison is true; includes boundary-value tests to determine inclusion.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Verification by substitution is a useful error-check strategy across all solving contexts.

in_depth.label: Equivalent linear inequalities
in_depth.description: Identifies when two inequalities have the same solution set; applies valid transformations with special care that multiplying or dividing by a negative reverses direction.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Equivalence reasoning under transformation is directly required when rewriting domain or range constraints.

in_depth.label: Addition property of inequality
in_depth.description: Adds the same quantity to both sides of an inequality without changing direction; used to undo a subtracted constant.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Routine solving step; parallel to equation property with no direction change involved.

in_depth.label: Subtraction property of inequality
in_depth.description: Subtracts the same quantity from both sides of an inequality without changing direction; used to undo an added constant.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Routine solving step; parallel to addition property.

umbrellas.label: Linear Inequality Foundations: Multiplication/Division and Representation
umbrellas.description: Multiplication and division properties of inequality (positive and negative), boundary values, number-line representation, and interval notation. NOTE: interval notation converting an inequality to bracket/parenthesis form is canonical in functions_and_graphs (F7); the keyword below is retained for DB row continuity only and should not generate problems that duplicate fg_interval_notation.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Sign-reversal properties and boundary/number-line representation are the highest-yield pieces of inequality foundations; interval notation is high indirect yield but owned by F7.

in_depth.label: Positive multiplication property of inequality
in_depth.description: Multiplying both sides by the same positive number preserves the inequality direction; used to undo division by a positive number.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Preservation rule is the contrast case that makes the negative reversal rule meaningful.

in_depth.label: Positive division property of inequality
in_depth.description: Dividing both sides by the same positive number preserves the inequality direction; used to isolate the variable when the coefficient is positive.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Most-common final step in inequality solving when the coefficient is positive; no reversal needed.

in_depth.label: Negative multiplication property of inequality
in_depth.description: Multiplying both sides by a negative number reverses the inequality direction; one of the most important distinctions between equation and inequality solving.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Sign-reversal rule is the most tested inequality-specific concept; errors here propagate into compound-inequality and absolute-value work.

in_depth.label: Negative division property of inequality
in_depth.description: Dividing both sides by a negative number reverses the inequality direction; used to isolate the variable when the coefficient is negative.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Most frequently triggered reversal scenario; persistent error source across all multi-step inequality solving.

in_depth.label: Inequality boundary values
in_depth.description: Identifies the boundary value of an inequality as the point where the related equation is true; determines whether that boundary is included based on the symbol.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Boundary-value identification is prerequisite for reading domain endpoints in rational and radical function graphs.

in_depth.label: Number-line representations of inequalities
in_depth.description: Graphs one-variable inequality solutions on a number line using open or closed endpoints and correct shading direction; limited to one-variable, not coordinate-plane.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Number-line graphing is a standard solution-representation skill tested in early algebra contexts.

in_depth.label: Interval notation for inequalities (DB row — scope redirected to F7)
in_depth.description: MECE NOTE: The canonical home for converting inequalities to bracket/parenthesis interval notation is functions_and_graphs (F7) under fg_interval_notation_from_inequality. This keyword is retained here solely to preserve its source_learn_keyword_id DB mapping. Problem generation for converting a single inequality such as 2 ≤ x < 5 to interval notation belongs to F7; this row should map to that concept in the student-facing system. F3's interval-notation instruction is covered by open_vs_closed_endpoint_meaning, infinity_endpoint_notation, and number_line_representation_inequalities in the interpretation umbrella.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Retained for DB row continuity only; active yield is assigned to the F7 canonical keyword. Set near zero to suppress generation.

umbrellas.label: One-Step Linear Inequalities
umbrellas.description: Linear inequalities solvable with a single inverse operation, covering addition, subtraction, positive and negative multiplication/division, fractions, and decimals. Correct sign-reversal decisions are the central skill.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: One-step inequality solving establishes the sign-reversal rule in its simplest form; the negative-coefficient cases are the highest-diagnostic-value items.

in_depth.label: One-step inequalities with addition
in_depth.description: Solves x + a < b or x + a > b by subtracting the constant from both sides with no reversal; tests addition case in inequality context.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Elementary case; no reversal involved and not independently assessed at AP level.

in_depth.label: One-step inequalities with subtraction
in_depth.description: Solves x − a < b or x − a > b by adding the constant with no reversal; tests subtraction case in inequality context.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Elementary case; parallel to addition.

in_depth.label: One-step inequalities with positive multiplication
in_depth.description: Solves ax < b with a > 0 by dividing by a positive coefficient without reversing; contrasts with the negative-coefficient case.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Establishes the no-reversal baseline for contrasting with the reversal rule.

in_depth.label: One-step inequalities with negative multiplication
in_depth.description: Solves −ax < b by dividing by a negative coefficient and reversing the symbol; isolates the one-step reversal scenario.
in_depth.yield_rationale: The minimal case where sign reversal is required; high diagnostic value for catching the most common inequality error.

in_depth.label: One-step inequalities with positive division
in_depth.description: Solves x/a < b with a > 0 by multiplying by a positive divisor without reversing; tests the positive-divisor no-reversal case.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Standard clearing-a-fraction-divisor step; no reversal involved.

in_depth.label: One-step inequalities with negative division
in_depth.description: Solves x/(−a) < b by multiplying by a negative divisor and reversing the symbol; isolates the negative-divisor reversal scenario.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Negative-divisor case is slightly less common but equally important as negative-coefficient multiplication for reversal recognition.

in_depth.label: One-step inequalities with fraction coefficients
in_depth.description: Solves inequalities with fractional coefficients by undoing the fraction multiplier; reversal required only if the multiplier or resulting divisor is negative.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Fraction-coefficient inequalities appear in domain/range analysis involving fractional function parameters.

in_depth.label: One-step inequalities with decimal coefficients
in_depth.description: Solves inequalities with decimal coefficients by dividing, with reversal only for negative coefficients; tests decimal arithmetic in inequality context.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Decimal inequality solving appears in applied model constraints; moderate direct frequency.

umbrellas.label: Two-Step Linear Inequalities
umbrellas.description: Linear inequalities requiring two inverse operations, with sign-reversal decisions based on whether the final operation involves a negative divisor or coefficient. Covers all coefficient types and boundary inclusion.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Two-step negative-coefficient case is the canonical inequality problem; fraction and decimal coefficient cases extend to domain-restriction analysis; boundary inclusion links directly to interval notation.

in_depth.label: Two-step inequalities with positive coefficients
in_depth.description: Solves ax + b < c with a > 0 by removing the constant term and then dividing by a positive coefficient without reversing the symbol.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Standard two-step inequality; no reversal needed but must sequence operations correctly.

in_depth.label: Two-step inequalities with negative coefficients
in_depth.description: Solves ax + b < c with a < 0 by removing the constant and then dividing by a negative coefficient with reversal; tests the most common reversal scenario.
in_depth.yield_rationale: The two-step negative-coefficient case is the canonical inequality problem testing reversal; highest-yield in this umbrella.

in_depth.label: Two-step inequalities with variable divided by a positive constant
in_depth.description: Solves x/a + b < c with a > 0 by isolating the quotient term and multiplying by a positive divisor; no reversal needed.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Quotient-variable inequality structure; positive divisor preserves direction.

in_depth.label: Two-step inequalities with variable divided by a negative constant
in_depth.description: Solves x/(−a) + b < c by isolating the quotient term and multiplying by a negative divisor with reversal; tests reversal in the quotient-variable setting.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Negative-divisor reversal in a two-step context is a targeted error type.

in_depth.label: Two-step inequalities with fraction coefficients
in_depth.description: Solves two-step inequalities with fractional variable coefficients; symbol reversal depends only on the sign of the fractional coefficient, not on the denominator magnitude.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Fractional-coefficient inequality solving extends to domain-restriction problems in function modeling.

in_depth.label: Two-step inequalities with decimal coefficients
in_depth.description: Solves two-step inequalities with decimal coefficients using decimal arithmetic or clearing; reversal depends only on the sign of the final divisor.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Decimal two-step inequalities appear in applied constraint modeling.

in_depth.label: Boundary inclusion in two-step inequalities
in_depth.description: Determines whether the solved boundary value is included in the solution based on the original inequality symbol; maps to open vs. closed endpoints in graphical and interval representations.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Endpoint-inclusion identification is prerequisite for writing domain and range in interval notation throughout AP Precalc.

in_depth.label: Inequality symbol reversal errors
in_depth.description: Recognizes and corrects reversal mistakes: the symbol reverses only when multiplying or dividing by a negative, and never for addition or subtraction operations.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Error-recognition items are high diagnostic value for detecting the most common inequality misconception.

umbrellas.label: Multi-Step Linear Inequalities: Distribution and Collection
umbrellas.description: Linear inequalities requiring distribution, like-term collection on one or both sides, and variables on both sides, combined with correct sign-reversal handling.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Multi-step inequality fluency supports domain/range reasoning for rational and radical functions; variables-on-both-sides is the standard form for comparing two function values over a domain interval.

in_depth.label: Linear inequalities requiring like-term collection on one side
in_depth.description: Combines like terms on one side before isolating the variable; mirrors multi-step equation structure while preserving inequality direction unless negative division follows.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Like-term collection before inequality isolation is a moderate-frequency skill in constraint analysis.

in_depth.label: Linear inequalities requiring distribution on one side
in_depth.description: Expands a grouped expression on one side before solving; targets inequalities such as 3(x + 2) < 15 where distribution precedes isolation.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Distribution in inequality context; moderate frequency in applied constraint problems.

in_depth.label: Linear inequalities with subtracted parentheses
in_depth.description: Changes the sign of every term inside a subtracted group before solving; combines grouping errors with inequality direction logic.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Sign-change in subtracted groups is the same algebraic move required in trig identity rewriting.

in_depth.label: Linear inequalities with multiple grouped expressions
in_depth.description: Distributes across two or more grouped expressions before collecting terms and isolating; the most complex single-step inequality expansion.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Multiple-group expansion in inequalities is less common than in equations but appears in advanced applied constraint problems.

in_depth.label: Linear inequalities with variables on both sides
in_depth.description: Moves variable terms to one side and constants to the other before isolating; direction reversal depends only on any final negative-coefficient division step.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Variables-on-both-sides inequality is the standard form for comparing two function values over a domain interval.

in_depth.label: Linear inequalities requiring distribution on both sides
in_depth.description: Distributes grouped expressions on both sides before collecting and isolating; most complex distribution scenario in linear inequalities.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Both-sides distribution is tested less frequently than one-side but appears in comparison-of-function-expressions problems.

umbrellas.label: Multi-Step Linear Inequalities: Clearing and Special Cases
umbrellas.description: Linear inequalities requiring LCD-clearing of fraction coefficients, power-of-10 clearing of decimals, and identification of no-solution or all-real-number special cases.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Fraction-clearing in inequalities arises in domain constraints; no-solution and all-real special cases model impossible or unconstrained domains in function analysis.

in_depth.label: Multi-step linear inequalities with fraction coefficients
in_depth.description: Solves inequalities with fractional coefficients using fraction arithmetic or LCD clearing; symbol reversal occurs only for negative fractional-coefficient division.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Fraction coefficients in inequalities arise in domain restrictions on fractional function parameters.

in_depth.label: Clearing positive denominators in linear inequalities
in_depth.description: Multiplies every term by a positive LCD to clear constant fraction denominators without reversing; applies only when all denominators are positive constants.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Positive-LCD clearing is the standard strategy for simplifying fractional inequalities into integer form.

in_depth.label: Multi-step linear inequalities with decimal coefficients
in_depth.description: Solves multi-step inequalities with decimal coefficients directly or by clearing with a power of 10; reversal depends on the sign of the final divisor.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Decimal inequality solving appears in applied constraints; lower frequency than fraction forms.

in_depth.label: Clearing decimal coefficients in linear inequalities
in_depth.description: Multiplies by a positive power of 10 to eliminate decimal coefficients before solving; preserves inequality direction because the multiplier is positive.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Useful technique; less frequent than fraction-clearing in AP-level inequality problems.

in_depth.label: Linear inequalities with no solution
in_depth.description: Identifies that an inequality has no solution when variable terms cancel and a false constant comparison such as 0 > 5 results.
in_depth.yield_rationale: No-solution inequality recognition appears in domain analysis — for instance, identifying when a constraint on x is never satisfied.

in_depth.label: Linear inequalities true for all real numbers
in_depth.description: Identifies that an inequality is satisfied by all real numbers when simplification produces an always-true comparison such as 0 < 5.
in_depth.yield_rationale: All-real solution inequalities model unconstrained domains; recognizing them is relevant to domain analysis of polynomial and exponential functions.

umbrellas.label: Compound Linear Inequalities: Structure and Solving
umbrellas.description: Solution sets formed by combining two linear inequalities with AND or OR logic; covers AND/OR structure, bounded intervals, three-part solving, and compound inequalities from separate solutions.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Compound inequalities model domain restrictions and range constraints throughout AP Precalc; three-part inequalities appear when isolating a variable in a bounded domain.

in_depth.label: AND compound inequality structure
in_depth.description: Identifies an AND compound inequality as requiring simultaneous satisfaction of both parts; its solution set is the intersection of the two individual solution sets.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Intersection logic directly models bounded domain constraints such as −1 ≤ x ≤ 4.

in_depth.label: OR compound inequality structure
in_depth.description: Identifies an OR compound inequality as satisfied by values meeting at least one condition; its solution set is the union of the two individual solution sets.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Union logic models domain exclusion constraints such as x < −1 or x > 5 for rational functions.

in_depth.label: Bounded compound inequalities
in_depth.description: Represents a double-bounded solution set such as a < x < b with two endpoints; reads and writes three-part inequality notation.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Bounded interval form is the standard representation for restricted domains of sinusoidal and logarithmic models.

in_depth.label: Three-part linear inequality solving
in_depth.description: Solves a < Bx + C < d by applying the same operation to all three parts simultaneously to isolate the variable in the middle; targets forms where a middle expression must be isolated.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Three-part solving is the direct method for isolating a variable inside a bounded constraint; used in finding inverse domains and restricted sinusoidal inputs.

in_depth.label: Compound inequalities from separate solved inequalities
in_depth.description: Solves two separate linear inequalities and then combines their solution sets using AND or OR logic; targets the case where each sub-inequality needs independent solution steps.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Solving and combining two inequalities is the standard approach for finding feasible parameter ranges in function modeling.

in_depth.label: Endpoint inclusion in compound inequalities
in_depth.description: Determines whether each endpoint of a compound inequality solution is included or excluded based on the corresponding inequality symbol; maps to brackets vs. parentheses in interval notation.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Endpoint-inclusion accuracy in compound inequalities is directly required for writing domain intervals with mixed strict/inclusive bounds.

umbrellas.label: Compound Linear Inequalities: Set Operations and Notation
umbrellas.description: Interval intersection and union for compound inequalities, no-overlap and all-real special cases, interval notation, and number-line graph representations.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Interval intersection/union are the set operations behind expressing domain as the overlap of two restrictions; notation forms are used throughout AP Precalc domain/range analysis.

in_depth.label: Interval intersections for compound inequalities
in_depth.description: Finds the overlap region of two solution sets for an AND compound inequality; works with inequality notation, number lines, or interval notation.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Interval intersection is the set-operation behind expressing domain as the intersection of two restrictions.

in_depth.label: Interval unions for compound inequalities
in_depth.description: Combines two disjoint solution sets for an OR compound inequality; writes the union in interval notation using the union symbol or as two separate intervals.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Interval unions express domains of functions with excluded points such as vertical asymptote neighborhoods.

in_depth.label: Compound inequalities with no overlap
in_depth.description: Identifies that an AND compound inequality has no solution when the required intervals are disjoint (e.g., x < 2 and x > 5); represents this as the empty set.
in_depth.yield_rationale: No-overlap recognition connects to impossible domain constraints; moderate diagnostic value.

in_depth.label: Compound inequalities with all-real-number union
in_depth.description: Recognizes that an OR compound inequality whose combined intervals cover the entire real line has all real numbers as its solution set.
in_depth.yield_rationale: All-real union represents unconstrained domains; less frequent as a direct test question.

in_depth.label: Interval notation for compound inequalities
in_depth.description: Writes compound inequality solution sets using interval notation including intersections, unions, and bounded intervals; translates between inequality, number-line, and interval forms.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Interval notation for compound sets is the primary tool for expressing domain and range in AP Precalc; high indirect yield.

in_depth.label: Number-line graphs for compound inequalities
in_depth.description: Graphs compound inequality solutions using multiple shaded regions or bounded segments on a number line; reads and writes both AND (intersection) and OR (union) configurations.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Number-line graphing of compound sets is a representation skill tested in early algebra; less direct use at AP level.

umbrellas.label: Linear Equation and Inequality Interpretation
umbrellas.description: Interpreting solution types for linear equations and inequalities: distinguishing single-value, interval, empty, and all-real solutions; correctly using interval notation, endpoint types, and infinity conventions.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Solution-interpretation literacy directly feeds domain/range communication throughout AP Precalc; interval notation and empty-set/all-real conventions appear in every unit.

in_depth.label: Equation versus inequality solution type
in_depth.description: Distinguishes that a typical linear equation has a single-point solution while a linear inequality produces a ray or interval; applies to reading and interpreting solved problems.
in_depth.yield_rationale: This conceptual distinction is the bridge between equation-solving and domain/range reasoning.

in_depth.label: Boundary value versus solution region
in_depth.description: Distinguishes the boundary (cutoff point) from the complete solution region of an inequality; recognizes that the full solution includes all values on the correct side with possible boundary inclusion.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Boundary-vs-region clarity is required for identifying domain endpoints of rational and radical functions.

in_depth.label: Open versus closed endpoint meaning
in_depth.description: Connects strict inequalities to open endpoints (parentheses, hollow dot) and inclusive inequalities to closed endpoints (brackets, filled dot); applies consistently across notation systems.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Open/closed endpoint notation appears in every domain and range expression throughout AP Precalc; high indirect yield.

in_depth.label: Infinity endpoint notation
in_depth.description: Applies the rule that ∞ and −∞ always use parentheses in interval notation because they are directions rather than reachable values; targets the common bracket-with-infinity error.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Infinity parenthesis rule is one of the most commonly violated notation conventions in domain/range writing.

in_depth.label: Empty solution set notation
in_depth.description: Represents a contradiction (no-solution case) using the empty set symbol ∅ or the empty set notation; applies to both equations and compound inequalities.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Empty-set notation is needed whenever a model constraint or equation system has no valid solution.

in_depth.label: All real numbers solution notation
in_depth.description: Represents an identity equation or always-true inequality using all-real-number notation (ℝ or (−∞, ∞)); distinguishes from a single specific solution.
in_depth.yield_rationale: All-real-number domain notation is needed for polynomial and exponential functions whose domain is unrestricted.

in_depth.label: Invalid steps causing wrong linear solutions
in_depth.description: Diagnoses structural errors — applying an operation to only one side, incorrect distribution, or unnecessary symbol reversal — that produce invalid equation or inequality solutions.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Error diagnosis is a high-value skill for catching mistakes in function-equation solving steps in FRQ work.

in_depth.label: Reasonableness of linear solutions
in_depth.description: Evaluates whether a solved value or inequality is reasonable by substitution, sign checking, boundary testing, or structural comparison to the original problem.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Reasonableness checking is a communication and reasoning skill (MP3) assessed in FRQ justification parts.

umbrellas.label: Absolute Value Equations and Inequalities
umbrellas.description: Solving equations and inequalities involving absolute value expressions by splitting into two linear cases; interpreting solution sets as single points, intervals, or unions based on the comparison.
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Absolute value equations and inequalities appear in domain restriction and distance-based model contexts; they are the natural extension of compound-inequality notation and feed into piecewise function analysis.

in_depth.label: Absolute value equations via two-case split
in_depth.description: Solves |ax + b| = c by splitting into ax + b = c and ax + b = −c, then solving each linear equation; applies only when c ≥ 0.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Two-case split is the standard technique for absolute-value equations; reappears when solving equations derived from piecewise-defined functions.

in_depth.label: Absolute value equations with no solution
in_depth.description: Identifies that |expression| = c has no solution when c < 0, since absolute value is always nonneg; avoids erroneously splitting into two cases.
in_depth.yield_rationale: No-solution recognition for absolute value equations prevents a specific category of error in equation solving.

in_depth.label: Absolute value equations with single solution
in_depth.description: Recognizes that |expression| = 0 produces exactly one solution because both cases reduce to the same equation; distinguishes from the general two-solution case.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Zero-right-side boundary case; moderate diagnostic value.

in_depth.label: Absolute value inequalities with < or ≤
in_depth.description: Rewrites |ax + b| < c as the AND compound inequality −c < ax + b < c and solves for a bounded interval solution; applies when c > 0.
in_depth.yield_rationale: The AND-compound rewrite of absolute-value less-than directly exercises the three-part inequality solving already established in this category.

in_depth.label: Absolute value inequalities with > or ≥
in_depth.description: Rewrites |ax + b| > c as the OR compound inequality ax + b < −c or ax + b > c and solves for a union-of-rays solution; applies when c > 0.
in_depth.yield_rationale: The OR-compound rewrite of absolute-value greater-than reinforces union notation and is a direct application of compound-inequality logic to a new structure.

in_depth.label: Absolute value inequalities: trivially true or false
in_depth.description: Identifies that |expression| > c is true for all real numbers when c < 0, and |expression| < c has no solution when c ≤ 0; applies structural reasoning before splitting.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Special-case recognition prevents wasted steps; connects to no-solution and all-real cases already established in compound inequalities.

umbrellas.label: Linear Function Graphs and Slope
umbrellas.description: Graphing linear functions in slope-intercept and point-slope forms, computing slope from two points, interpreting slope as a rate of change, identifying slope and y-intercept from a graph, and classifying horizontal, vertical, parallel, and perpendicular lines. Direct prerequisite for CED topics 1.2 (AROC as slope) and 1.3 (constant rate of change identifies a linear function).
umbrellas.yield_rationale: Slope is the foundational rate-of-change concept: AP Precalc 1.2 defines AROC as slope of a secant line and 1.3 characterizes linear functions by constant rate of change. Fluency with slope-intercept and point-slope forms is also required for tangent-line approximation in Calc AB (C4).

in_depth.label: Slope from two points
in_depth.description: Computes slope as rise over run using m = (y₂ − y₁)/(x₂ − x₁); handles positive, negative, zero, and undefined slope cases.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Slope computation from coordinates is directly exercised in AROC calculations and secant-line analysis throughout AP Precalc and Calc AB.

in_depth.label: Slope as a rate of change
in_depth.description: Interprets the slope of a linear function as the constant rate at which the output changes per unit of input; connects numerical slope to verbal descriptions of change.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Rate-of-change interpretation of slope is the conceptual bridge to AROC (AP Precalc 1.2) and to the derivative as instantaneous rate of change (Calc AB).

in_depth.label: Graphing in slope-intercept form
in_depth.description: Identifies m and b in y = mx + b and uses them to graph the line: plots the y-intercept and applies slope as rise/run to find additional points.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Slope-intercept graphing is prerequisite for interpreting linear function behavior, y-intercept meaning, and transformations in AP Precalc Unit 1.

in_depth.label: Point-slope form of a linear equation
in_depth.description: Writes and uses the equation y − y₁ = m(x − x₁) given a point and slope; converts between point-slope and slope-intercept forms.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Point-slope form is the natural output of the AROC/secant-slope computation and is the standard form for tangent-line equations in Calc AB.

in_depth.label: Reading slope and y-intercept from a graph
in_depth.description: Identifies the slope (steepness and direction) and y-intercept of a linear function directly from its graph; selects two lattice-point coordinates to compute slope accurately.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Graph-reading for slope and intercept is tested in AP Precalc graphical representation tasks and in Calc AB graph-based AROC problems.

in_depth.label: Horizontal and vertical lines
in_depth.description: Distinguishes equations of horizontal lines (y = k, slope 0) from vertical lines (x = h, undefined slope); connects to zero and undefined rate of change.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Horizontal and vertical line cases arise in domain/range analysis and in identifying constant functions vs. non-functions.

in_depth.label: Parallel and perpendicular slope relationships
in_depth.description: Identifies that parallel lines have equal slopes and perpendicular lines have slopes that are negative reciprocals; applies these relationships to write equations of related lines.
in_depth.yield_rationale: Parallel/perpendicular slope relationships appear in geometric applications and in normal-line construction in Calc AB.
